name: STEMI - Right Ventricular
identifyBy: ST elevation >=1mm in right-sided lead V4R (most sensitive and specific)
Usually occurs with inferior STEMI (RCA occlusion) - look for II, III, aVF elevation
Classic triad: hypotension + elevated JVP + clear lung fields
ST elevation may extend to V3R-V6R on right-sided ECG
ST elevation in V1 with inferior STEMI also suggests RV involvement

measurements.stSegment: Elevation V3R-V6R (right-sided leads) + inferior II, III, aVF

management: Inferior STEMI protocol - always check right-sided leads
AVOID GTN and other vasodilators - RV is preload-dependent
IV fluid challenge 250-500mL - RV infarct is preload-dependent (BP often improves)
Dobutamine may support RV function if fluid unresponsive
pearls: GTN is absolutely contraindicated - causes profound hypotension in RV infarct
The right ventricle has little collateral circulation - it relies entirely on the RCA
V4R ST elevation resolves within 12-18 hours - do right-sided ECG early if inferior STEMI suspected
Kussmaul's sign (JVP rising on inspiration) may be present - RV failure finding
RV infarct triad: hypotension + raised JVP + clear lung fields (not heart failure)

qaNotes: Ischaemia/STEMI morphology retained from source content; verify affected leads in multi-lead view.
